content: The single most painful lesson I learned after spending months writing recursive descent parsers in hand-written Rust was the illusion of control you get when you forget about error recovery until it's too late. When you first dive into Chumsky, that sweet interface makes parsing feel like magic—you call `parse_expression`, and if the grammar is clean, you get an AST back with perfect spans via the Span trait. But as soon as a user types something invalid, your parser just dies silently or panics on a simple assertion because you never implemented any recovery logic at all. Beginners make this mistake by treating parsing like a validation step rather than a state machine; they assume if `parse` returns an error, that's the end of the story, but in reality, a robust parser must recover from errors and continue consuming tokens to build as much of the AST as possible before the input ends. The reason beginners miss this is because Chumsky's API is so high-level that it hides the underlying state machine complexity; you don't see where the cursor is or what token was last consumed, so you can't easily implement backtracking or skipping logic without manually managing indices in a vector. Instead of trying to build your own recovery loop with manual index tracking, leverage Chumsky's built-in error handling by wrapping your parser rules in `Parser::try_parse` and attaching custom error messages via the Error trait implementation, but go further by implementing a post-processing phase where you reconstruct the AST using only successfully parsed segments. For example, imagine parsing a simple expression language with integers and operators; when you encounter a syntax error like an operator missing its right operand, instead of stopping immediately, your parser should skip ahead to find the next valid token—say, a number or another operator—and append that segment to the partial AST before declaring failure at the end. This approach ensures that even if the user makes typos, they get back a partially useful structure rather than an empty result, which is far more helpful for interactive shells and code editors. Another critical insight is how spans work: every token you parse should carry its start and end byte positions so you can map errors directly to the source file, but beginners often forget that error recovery breaks continuity of these spans; if you skip tokens during recovery, your span calculations become unreliable unless you carefully track offsets for each recovered segment. My recommendation is to combine Chumsky's `Span` trait with a custom recovery strategy where you explicitly log skipped ranges in an AST node tagged as partial or incomplete, allowing downstream tools like linters or formatters to handle these gracefully without crashing. Don't try to emulate error recovery manually by looping through your token stream and checking conditions; instead, design your grammar rules so that common errors map naturally into Chumsky's `Result` type while you intercept failures at the top level to trigger a fallback reconstruction algorithm based on the last known good state plus any recoverable segments. This shifts your mindset from writing brittle parsers to building resilient ones where bad input produces meaningful output rather than noise, and it makes a world of difference when you're actually deploying this in production systems where user input is rarely perfect.
